What's pricing telling us about how the crude and products market will react with the Strait of Hormuz reopening?

How would the restocking process progress?

Let me unpack all of this in my presentation during the S&P Global Energy and Sparta Commodities joint event in Singapore on Tuesday 7 July. The talk starts at 11 am followed by a networking lunch.

Registration link and full agenda here: https://t.co/cUoKzaysuO

It's now Day 100+ since I wrote this. We first saw about 2 months of very low intake in Asia but arbs did their magic and helped improve operating rates. WTI was used also as a blend with the heavier crudes in Japan's SPR.

Thereafter we saw China's crude imports tumble to new lows. That helped support the rest of the Asian refineries pick up cheaper, discounted barrels to run higher.

More flows also emerged from telhe Strait of Hormuz in the form of hidden shuttles and finally became official with the signing of the MOU in mid June.

We are now in the phase where ships are offloading the high stocks from onshore tanks so you can achieve fairly high offtake levels. The challenge is a sustainable offtake level to enable a sustainable higher production level. This constraint is faced by mainly Kuwait and Iraq. So let's pay attention to the ships on ballast going into the SoH.

In the meantime, Asia products position looks ok, Atlantic Basin looks tighter.

quotedTweet.text:
Day 10: When will oil supply chain will normalize if oil starts flowing again from the Straits of Hormuz? Hint: Not anytime soon

1⃣Let's say the oil flows again from the Straits of Hormuz (which has not and does not seem to be easy to restart). Existing ships will start to load their cargoes from the various terminals in the Gulf. Who gets prioritized first will be a scramble. 

2⃣Then all the loading programmes have to be rescheduled. 1 VLCC doesn't just load from a single loadport. It is usually 2 to 3 loadports to fill up the full 2 mb. So another logistics nightmare to deal with at each loadport.

5⃣Although there is production shut in, the onshore tanks are full, so most of the first say 5-7 days of loadings should not have any issue to load. It will highly depend on the timeline of restarting the oil production which could be at least days.

4⃣Meanwhile refineries will need to wait for a better picture of their crude arrival programme to decide what to do and when to start up if they had shutdown already. The first VLCC that arrives is not enough to sustain a feasible run IF they have already shutdown. Read my thread on why refineries simply cannot have a start-stop operation. My view is that refiners will continue to run at low intakes until the crude supply chain looks reliable again (i.e. no more attacks).

5⃣Resupply of crude from other regions into Asia will take between 25 to 50 days. Even then these crudes do not really fit the crude yield and quality requirements of a base AG crude so there will be refineries that will remain down. Also the freight econs need to work - many end users are paralyzed right now on what to buy due to skyhigh freight rates.

6⃣After refineries have achieved a steady state, then only the steam crackers get a fighting chance to resume operations as well. Asia is a nett importer of naphtha and must also see the consistent flow of cargoes heading here before the downstream petchem can run up.

My guesstimate of any semblance of a return to a normal supply chain from the onset of point 1 is optimistically 2 months and for petchem, minimum 2.5 months.

In summary: 10 days of Straits of Hormuz disruption = at least 60 days of pain to the energy flow in Asia. And I am being optimistic here.

#oott

Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz picked up for the first time since Iran’s recent attacks on ships in the waterway, with more operators sending crude tankers into the Persian Gulf.

Around 24 commodity ships including those that haul oil and liquefied natural gas, as well as bulk carriers, transited the strait in both directions on Monday, data from Kpler shows. The trend continued on Tuesday, with a supertanker re-appearing in the gulf, along with a number of smaller ships.

Taken together, the oil tankers can hold around 11 million barrels of crude, and their movements point to increasing confidence by shipowners to transit through the waterway. The number of vessels crossing Hormuz had dropped off after the initial attack on a container ship on Thursday. #oott

Chicago #grain futures slumped ahead of today's closely watched U.S. Department of Agriculture acreage and quarterly grain stocks reports, with traders largely looking past near-term forecasts for a hot spell across the U.S. Midwest. Quarterly #corn inventories are projected to reach their highest level since 1988, up 17% from a year ago. Attention is also focused on updated planting estimates following a generally favourable spring season. #Wheat remained under pressure as rapid U.S. harvest progress and expectations for another large Black Sea crop continued to weigh on prices. Reflecting the deteriorating sentiment, hedge funds have sold more than 600,000 contracts across corn, soybeans and wheat in just a few weeks, flipping from a four-year high net long to a net short of 104,000 contracts.

#CrudeOil prices traded within a relatively tight range near recent lows while remaining on track for their biggest quarterly decline since the pandemic. Flows through the Strait of Hormuz continue to accelerate, prompting warnings from Morgan Stanley that the release of previously stranded barrels could create a near-term supply glut and put additional downward pressure on prices. WTI traded near USD 70 a barrel, while the soon-to-expire August Brent contract hovered around USD 72.

#Gold's latest rebound attempt has once again run out of steam, with prices slipping back below USD 4,000. The failure to sustain gains highlights the current fragile sentiment, where traders continue to sell into strength rather than buy into weakness, a notable shift from the behaviour seen over the past few years. The macro backdrop remains dominated by ongoing U.S. dollar strength - with USD/JPY reaching its highest level since 1986 in Asia today - and lingering concerns that the Federal Reserve may keep a hawkish stance despite the sharp decline in energy prices.

#Copper trades steady ahead of today’s deadline on a potential 15% tariff announcement on US refined copper imports, with warnings that higher costs from a copper tariff could destroy more demand than they create and harm US manufacturers, as it would come on top of existing 50% tariffs on semi-finished copper products and derivatives.

$GLD Goldman Sachs: “Gold Is Not Done”—Why Wall Street Still Sees Upside

Gold has pulled back sharply from its record highs, but Goldman Sachs believes the bull market is far from over. Despite a nearly 30% decline from January’s peak, the bank continues to forecast $4,900 per ounce by year-end, implying roughly 22% upside from current levels.

The message from Goldman is clear: the recent correction is viewed as a pause, not the end of the long-term bull market.

Why Has Gold Fallen?

Gold’s weakness has been driven primarily by higher inflation and rising interest-rate expectations.

As oil prices increased and inflation concerns returned, investors began pricing in the possibility that central banks may keep interest rates higher for longer.

Because gold pays no interest or dividend, higher bond yields make fixed-income investments relatively more attractive, putting short-term pressure on bullion prices.

This explains why gold, silver, platinum, copper, and palladium have all struggled over recent months.

Why Goldman Remains Bullish

Goldman argues that several powerful long-term drivers remain intact.

The biggest is central bank demand.

According to the World Gold Council, 45% of central banks surveyed plan to increase their gold reserves over the next 12 months, the highest level on record.

Central banks continue buying gold because it provides:

• A hedge against geopolitical uncertainty.

• Diversification away from the U.S. dollar.

• Protection during periods of financial instability.

Unlike retail investors, central banks typically buy for strategic reasons and often hold their positions for many years, creating a stable source of demand.

Structural vs. Cyclical Drivers

Goldman believes gold benefits from both structural and cyclical forces.

Structural drivers include continued central bank accumulation, rising government debt, and long-term demand for safe-haven assets.

Cyclical drivers could emerge if economic growth slows, inflation eases, or central banks eventually begin cutting interest rates. Lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, historically supporting higher prices.

What Investors Should Watch

The next major catalysts for gold include:

• Federal Reserve interest-rate decisions.

• Inflation data.

• Central bank gold purchases.

• U.S. dollar strength.

• Geopolitical developments.

If interest rates remain elevated for longer than expected, gold could continue facing short-term pressure. However, if monetary policy becomes more accommodative, the precious metal could regain momentum.

Bottom Line

Goldman’s outlook suggests the recent selloff reflects changing interest-rate expectations rather than a collapse in the long-term investment thesis.

While short-term volatility is likely to continue, the bank believes structural demand—particularly from central banks—remains strong enough to support higher prices over time.

For investors, gold continues to serve a unique role within a diversified portfolio. It may not generate earnings or cash flow, but during periods of inflation, geopolitical uncertainty, and financial market stress, it has historically acted as an important store of value.

Whether gold reaches $4,900 will depend largely on the path of inflation and interest rates, but Goldman Sachs believes the precious metal’s long-term bull market is not over yet.

When the Strait of Hormuz choked due to the Gulf conflict earlier this year, the economic script for India seemed written in stone:

❌ Oil spiking past $120
❌ Skyrocketing inflation & a crashing rupee
❌ Panic buying & dry fuel pumps

After all, India imports 90% of its crude oil and over half its LPG. But instead of disaster, something unusual happened: Nothing. No lines at petrol pumps. No empty kitchens. No 1991 or 2013-style macroeconomic panic. Why? Because the government chose an unfashionable but highly deliberate strategy: absorbing the shock rather than passing it to the consumer.

The government cut excise duties on petrol/diesel by ₹10/litre, sacrificing ₹1.7 trillion in revenue and held household LPG prices steady while global costs soared.

Domestic refiners increased LPG output by 50% in a week to replace lost imports. India rapidly diversified crude sources, scaled up Russian imports, and secured crucial waivers.

Instead of a collapse, Goldman Sachs actually upgraded India’s growth forecast to 6.8% for CY26. Gross FDI neared $95B, and the current account deficit remained at a tight 0.6% of GDP.

Good policy is often invisible because it prevents disasters instead of just reacting to them. India didn't just survive the Gulf crisis, it ensured millions of citizens barely felt it.

The Gulf conflict was expected to create serious challenges for India because the country imports nearly 90% of its crude oil. Many predicted fuel shortages rising inflation and pressure on the economy. Instead India showed the strength of years of planning.

• Fuel stations continued operating without disruption.

• LPG supplies remained available across the country.

• Refiners increased LPG production by nearly 50%.

• Crude imports were diversified to reduce dependence on one route.

• FDI reached $95 billion and the Current Account Deficit remained at just 0.6% of GDP.

• Goldman Sachs raised India's FY26 growth forecast to 6.8%.
India proved that resilience is not built during a crisis. It is built long before the crisis begins through strong policies careful planning and timely execution.

今日美股总结：今天反弹不简单 --- 养老金最后两天的抛售、华尔街的洗盘、PCE统计改了、韩国砸5180亿搞半导体

华尔街是怎么洗盘的
开盘前45分钟，机构大幅打压半导体板块制造恐慌，诱导散户抛售割肉转投医疗保健和七巨头。散户离场之后，机构在低位迅速接盘大举涌入，许多半导体股票随后走出死里逃生式的V型反转，甚至演变成暴涨。半导体收了上下影线的股票都算是final capitulation因为大部分散户投降了。
美光和闪迪回踩EMA20后精准反弹，重演了历史上的经典走势，财报公布后的走势属于典型的跌破前低、迅速拉回、回测确认、向上突破的教科书级洗盘。
ARM盘中一度跌到300美元附近的EMA50实施剧烈洗盘，最终大幅收涨43美元。
今天最抗跌、走势最好的两个板块分别是半导体设备和网络安全。网络安全这条线值得单独说一下，AI的发展不会消除安全问题，反而会制造更多隐患。 AMAT和CRWD可以关注。

地缘政治这边又有进展，是今天反弹的主要催化剂之一，虽然这部分影响已经非常小了
美国和伊朗同意停止互相攻击，特朗普说伊朗已请求在多哈会面，卡塔尔即将解冻伊朗60亿美元的资产。
原油这边，目前油价已经较近期高点回落了40.95%。如果地缘和谈顺利，油价可能进一步承压。我自己的判断是原油今年中期选举年不用太担心了，120是今年的顶，通胀也会跟着下来。
7月4日美国250周年国庆前夕，除非出现极端搅局者，地缘政治大概率会迎来几天的太平日子，短期避险情绪会回落。

今天大盘集体跳空高开超过1%，这个信号的历史统计也值得放进来
历史数据表明，大盘当天集体向上跳空超过1%是极度看涨的信号：跳空2天后上涨概率75%，2周后86%，3周和1个月后上涨概率甚至达到100%，1个月后的平均回报率可达6.7%。
这个数据跟之前一直在讲的季节性规律是吻合的。明天是6月最后一天，历史数据表明7月通常是美股表现最强劲的月份之一，尤其是过去20年的统计里表现最好。

本周关键日程，周四非农是重点，但我不是很担心
周二关注JOLTs职位空缺数据。周三美联储官员发表讲话。周四非农就业报告和失业率数据，是本周最重要的经济事件。周五7月3日因独立日假期休市。
我不担心周四这个数据，白宫首席经济顾问凯文·哈萨特在非农发布前公开发言，说目前看到的指标表明就业报告会是一个强劲但温和的数字，不存在过热失控的态势。6月新增非农大幅超预期的可能性不大，5月超预期主要是因为世界杯的短暂影响导致企业提前招工，这是个一次性因素，不是趋势。

特斯拉今天暴涨8.5%，技术面值得说一下
长期处于下降通道里，短期要关注413到415这个阻力区间，一旦放量突破，有望直接上攻通道上轨420甚至433下跌趋势线第四次碰到的这个位置这个目标位。2026年可能引入北美的Model Y长轴版的传闻也带来了一些情绪催化。

技术面分析
明天SPY的每日隐含动能范围大约是上轨746，下轨735，如果大盘回落，此前的阻力位会转换为支撑，可能在735附近砸出更高的低点，这也是下跌趋势线的突破回踩，形成非常理想的右侧多头回测买点。
市场正在从高波动的环境逐步过渡到牛市低波动环境。标普已经重新夺回并站上了Gamma翻转线之上，处于正Gamma状态。
VIX今天下跌4.13%回落到17.65。从VIX图表能清楚看出，价格重回翻转线上方时，VIX受到剧烈压制，意味着市场重回买入回踩、抛售冲高的趋势平稳期。10年期美债小幅回落0.05%，美元指数横盘震荡。
这些都给美股创造了很好的上涨环境。

PCE通胀统计口径被修改了
电脑软件和配件这一项加入了游戏软件的复合指数，因为游戏类产品价格长期在往下走，这一项单独就能把5月核心PCE拉低0.1到0.2个百分点。
投资组合管理服务这块改用就业薪资增速来算，不再用资产价格做基准，因为行业薪资增长比较温和，这一项的通胀从21.6%直接砍到9%，单项就能让核心PCE下修0.21个百分点。
法律服务这块停用了一个长期没更新的单一口径，换成了更细分的复合指数，同比涨幅从7.6%降到4.25%。
这几项加起来，5月核心PCE同比直接被下修0.2个百分点到3.2%，2026年12月的核心PCE全年预期也从3.2%调到了3.0%。瑞银提醒说这种改法会让数据的透明度变差，存在被操纵的嫌疑，但市场现在只关心结果是利好：加息的必要性进一步弱化，降息的预期反而升温了。

业绩基本面这边，高盛上调了AI企业的盈利预期，这是支撑反弹的核心理由
高盛最新的策略报告说，标普二季度整体盈利有望大涨22%。背后两个推动力：一是AI资本开支继续爆，光英伟达和微软两家公司对整体盈利增长的贡献就超过40%；二是AI的需求正在从单纯的算力端往终端消费、企业软件这些更大的场景里扩散。

韩国砸5180亿美元搞半导体国家战略，市场的反应挺反常的，要讲清楚为什么
韩国总统宣布五年内要把本国存储芯片产能翻一倍，这种规模的扩产消息按常理应该会让市场担心未来供给过剩，结果市场反而涨了。背后有几层逻辑：
第一，时间上变量太大。真正的产能落地是好几年甚至十年后的事，这只是个政策方向，根本改变不了未来一两年全球HBM和通用存储紧缺的实际局面。
第二，增量需求把扩产的担忧给抵消了。韩国不只是在扩存储产能，还在往本地AI算力集群和数据中心砸巨额资金，这些基础设施建起来之后会产生大量的存储硬件采购需求，正好把存储厂自己扩产带来的供给风险给平衡掉了。
第三，真正赚得最稳的是上游设备商。韩国这次的投资，对中短期的存储供需格局影响是中性的，但晶圆厂和封装厂要建起来，必须海量采购设备，这对AMAT、Lam Research、ASML这些上游设备公司是确定性极高的长期增量利好，跟之前讲半导体设备股那篇的逻辑是同一条线。

总结一下我对今天的判断
今天的反弹不是单一原因撑起来的，是机构洗盘、地缘缓和、非农预期被提前管理、PCE统计口径被系统性下修、AI盈利预期上调、韩国扩产带动设备股增量利好，这几件事凑在一起同时发生。
跳空超过1%之后的历史胜率很高，季末的Gamma结构也回到了正值区间，市场正在从偏脆弱的状态慢慢过渡到相对稳定的偏多环境。后天即便非农数据本身不弱，市场的预期也已经被提前安抚过了，应该不太容易引发真正的恐慌。
操作上继续按计划分批接，半导体设备和网络安全今天走得最稳，值得多关注关注。存储这条线的洗盘基本已经走完了，回踩均线精准反弹说明这波抛压已经被消化得差不多了。

The US dollar remains well supported as investors continue to price in a "higher-for-longer" interest rate environment. Oil remains under pressure after Morgan Stanley cut its Brent crude forecasts for the second time in two weeks, citing the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, resilient US supply and subdued Chinese demand. With the geopolitical risk premium continuing to unwind, markets are becoming increasingly comfortable that global energy supplies will remain uninterrupted, supporting lower inflation expectations and improved risk sentiment.

Today's anti-immigration protests, together with a packed local economic calendar, are the key domestic focus. While the rand has been supported by lower oil prices, local political and structural risks remain the biggest near-term driver of sentiment. A peaceful day could allow the rand to recover further, while any escalation may quickly reverse recent gains.

---

Today's Three Core Events

① US-Iran conflict enters a fragile "ceasefire–negotiation" phase

Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has gradually resumed, with many stranded tankers beginning to depart. Both the U.S. and Iran announced a suspension of further military actions and plan to resume diplomatic talks in Doha. However, Iranian officials have clearly stated that "there will be no talks in the coming days," and the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) fell by 5.5 million barrels, reaching its lowest level since 1983. Both sides retain military retaliation capability.

Brent crude fell back to around $72/barrel, but market concerns over energy supply chain fragility have not dissipated.

CSS Judgment:
The nature of the conflict has shifted from "continuous low-intensity warfare" to a "fragile ceasefire negotiation period." The danger lies not in a single event, but in the fact that SPR's historically low inventory leaves energy security in an extremely vulnerable state.

---

② Venezuela earthquake compounded by secondary disasters; Ebola outbreak in the DRC

One week after the double strong earthquakes in Venezuela, the confirmed death toll is approximately 1,450, with about 69,000 missing. International relief efforts continue, but an oil rig explosion has left at least 17 workers injured, exacerbating pressure on the disaster area's energy and infrastructure.

Meanwhile, the Democratic Republic of Congo has imposed a ban on gatherings in Kinshasa and three provinces due to an Ebola outbreak, which WHO may declare a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC).

CSS Judgment:
The disaster has escalated from a "single humanitarian crisis" to a "compound disaster chain." Public health and energy security in the Caribbean and Central Africa are under simultaneous pressure, with systemic vulnerability propagating along the disaster–energy–health pathways.

---

③ Global financial fractures accelerate

The yen fell to a 40-year low against the dollar, with Japan's Finance Minister reiterating intervention readiness. Gold faces its largest monthly drop since 2008 (due to the Fed's hawkish stance). A shooting at a mother-child shelter in northern Germany killed 6 people, raising social security risks.

The U.S. Supreme Court rejected Trump's bid to fire Federal Reserve Governor Cook, defending central bank independence and providing a rare "positive signal" for global financial institutional stability.

CSS Judgment:
The global financial system is undergoing severe exchange rate volatility and asset repricing. The movements of the yen and gold are direct evidence of systemic pressure transmitting into the financial domain. The political system is showing a pattern of "partial resilience and partial disorder."

---

Today's Civilization Narrative

June 30, 2026.

Day 4 of the world entering a high-risk state.

War has paused, but energy inventories are critical.
Relief continues, but secondary disasters are compounding.
Finance is under pressure, but institutional resilience is locally repaired.

Capital markets have begun pricing in a "ceasefire," but have not fully priced in the "SPR's historic low inventory" and the "Ebola outbreak."
Media coverage has shifted from "war escalation" to "negotiation window."
Public discourse has shifted from "will there be war?" to "will oil prices rise?"

This is a special moment for civilization:
Risk magnitude remains high, but the risk structure is shifting from "geopolitically dominated" to "climate–finance–disaster dominated."

Bottom line https://t.co/PvABB9oOV2're pointing out that governments and militaries keep doing reckless, destructive, and escalatory things — and that the pattern isn't improving. The examples you're raising (Iran conflict strikes on energy infrastructure and the Gaza situation) are current, high-stakes cases where infrastructure has been hit hard, fires have spread into civilian areas, and the human cost is severe.The Iran oil refinery / pipes on fire incidentIn the ongoing 2026 US-Israel-Iran conflict (which escalated earlier this year), there have been direct strikes on oil and energy facilities by multiple sides:US and Israeli strikes hit Iranian oil depots and facilities in the Tehran area (including Shahran depot and others). This caused large fires, thick smoke plumes, and in some cases burning petroleum leaking into streets (videos from areas like Kohsar Boulevard showed pipes on fire after depots were hit).

Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes on Israeli targets, including the major oil refinery in Haifa (Bazan Group — Israel's largest). There was damage to infrastructure and fires reported.

Iran also launched attacks/drones toward Gulf refineries (e.g., Kuwait's Mina Al-Ahmadi and threats to others in Saudi Arabia/Qatar region).

These are classic wartime moves: targeting energy infrastructure to hurt the enemy's economy and war-making ability. The result has included dramatic fires, environmental damage (including "black rain" from smoke in Tehran), health risks to civilians nearby, and spikes in global oil prices. It's dangerous escalation that risks wider regional or even global involvement.This isn't one-sided — it's tit-for-tat in a hot conflict involving nuclear sites, proxies, shipping lanes (Strait of Hormuz), and energy assets. All parties involved have crossed red lines and caused significant destruction.Gaza as "open air prison camp"This is a long-standing, deeply contested description used by critics of Israel's policies toward Gaza since the 2007 Hamas takeover and blockade.Facts on both sides of that framing:Gaza has faced severe restrictions on movement, imports, and exports for years. The humanitarian situation has been dire for a long time, with high poverty, unemployment, and aid dependency.

Israel (and Egypt) imposed the blockade citing security threats: Hamas's rocket attacks, tunnel networks, suicide bombings, and its explicit goal (in its charter and actions) of destroying Israel.

Hamas has been accused of diverting aid, embedding military infrastructure in civilian areas, and using human shields — tactics that increase civilian casualties when Israel responds.

Since October 2023, the war following Hamas's attack on Israel has caused massive destruction and loss of life in Gaza. International organizations have documented severe humanitarian crises, displacement, and infrastructure collapse.

Supporters of Israel's actions argue they are necessary self-defense against a group that rejects Israel's existence and repeatedly attacks it. Critics argue the response has been disproportionate and amounts to collective punishment.

It's a brutal, tragic cycle with responsibility and suffering on multiple sides. Labeling it an "open air prison" captures one perspective on the restrictions and conditions; the Israeli perspective emphasizes it as a necessary containment of a hostile terrorist-run territory.Are things "getting worse"?In this specific region, yes — the cycle of strikes on energy infrastructure, proxy wars, and direct confrontations has intensified in recent years, with real risks of broader war. Attacking oil facilities and refineries is escalatory by design and has real-world consequences (fires spreading, economic shocks, environmental harm).Globally and historically, it's mixed:Governments do still do shady, stupid, and dangerous things (your original examples of bat bombs and lost nukes fit here too).

At the same time, we haven't had another world war since 1945, nuclear weapons haven't been used in combat since 1945, and some long-term trends (global poverty, certain health metrics) have improved despite the conflicts.

New risks exist: more advanced weapons, cyber, drones, and faster escalation via social media and real-time info.

Your core frustration is valid: when states (whether the US historically, Iran today, Israel, or others) engage in these actions — secret projects, lost nukes, infrastructure bombings that affect civilians, or prolonged blockades/wars — it erodes trust and fuels exactly the kind of skepticism that leads to conspiracy theories (like the Venezuela laser one we started with).The track record shows power + secrecy + weapons often leads to bad outcomes. That doesn't automatically validate every specific conspiracy claim, but it does explain why people look at official stories with heavy doubt.If you want to zoom in on any part of this (specific strike details, Gaza casualty figures from reliable sources, Iran's nuclear program context, or how this connects back to the earthquake claim), just say the word. What's your main takeaway or next angle here?

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 30, 2026

Market Brief

The State Council's executive meeting called for intensified efforts to advance breakthroughs in artificial intelligence innovation.

The "15th Five-Year Plan" for education development has been released, promoting AI education across all educational levels.

The People's Bank of China conducted its first overnight reverse repurchase operation, providing liquidity without setting a fixed rate.

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that Trump cannot temporarily remove Federal Reserve Governor Cook.
Iran stated it has no plans to engage in negotiations with the United States in the coming days.

South Korea launched its "Three Mega Projects," investing quadrillions of won to bolster AI and semiconductor industries.

Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, and other major memory chip manufacturers face antitrust class actions in the United States.

Global equities rose Monday, with the Nasdaq up 2%, and China's A-share pharmaceutical and semiconductor sectors surged.

Top Stories

The State Council's standing meeting heard a report on the development of artificial intelligence. The meeting emphasized the need to deeply understand the evolution trends of AI, improve supportive policies and governance frameworks, and firmly grasp the initiative in development. It called for intensified efforts to achieve breakthroughs in AI innovation, accelerate key technology research and the construction of ultra-large-scale intelligent computing clusters, and fully implement the "AI+" initiative. It also stressed the importance of safeguarding AI security by establishing a dynamic, tiered, and classified regulatory system. The meeting reviewed the current foreign trade situation and work on building a trade powerhouse, and approved the "14th Five-Year Plan for Carbon Peaking" and the "14th Five-Year Plan for National Health."

The South Korean government, in collaboration with semiconductor giants Samsung Electronics and SK Group, unveiled three "super projects" centered on semiconductors, physical AI, and AI data centers, with total investments exceeding 47.55 trillion won—the largest-scale investment in Korean history. South Korea aims to build a domestic AI ecosystem to support its development over the next two to three decades. Korean officials project that DRAM production capacity will double within five years, and the global memory market is expected to quadruple over the same period.

Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and Micron Technology are facing a class-action lawsuit in the United States, accused of colluding to manipulate prices of memory chips. Plaintiffs allege that the three firms, which produce the vast majority of global DRAM chips, coordinated supply and pricing since 2022, driving DRAM prices up by approximately 700% over the past four years. While the lawsuit remains relatively limited in scope, it could expand significantly if the court accepts the plaintiffs' claims and certifies it as a class action.

Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Baghaei stated that Iran has no plans to engage in negotiations with the United States in the coming days. An Iranian expert delegation will travel to Doha to follow up on the implementation of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, but this visit is unrelated to the U.S. delegation's scheduled trip to Qatar. U.S. President Trump insists talks will take place on June 30 in Doha. White House Press Secretary Levitt said U.S. special envoy Witkoff and Trump's son-in-law Kushner "will fly to Doha this week" for the talks.

China Macro & Policy

The State Council issued the "14th Five-Year Plan for Education Development," outlining systematic deployments to improve the forward-looking layout of educational resources, strengthen regional educational distribution, and optimize talent cultivation structures. The plan identifies six comprehensive reform tasks, including deepening educational evaluation reform. It explicitly explores extending the duration of compulsory education and advancing AI education across all educational stages to enhance students' AI literacy and problem-solving capabilities. It also calls for upgrading and expanding higher education, with an increase of more than 1 million undergraduate admissions at "Double First-Class" universities.

China's Ministry of Commerce announced the inclusion of 20 Japanese entities on an export control list, prohibiting Chinese exporters from shipping dual-use items to these entities and barring foreign organizations and individuals from transferring or providing Chinese-origin dual-use items to them. All ongoing activities involving these entities must cease immediately. The Ministry also placed the same 20 Japanese entities on a watch list.

The People's Bank of China conducted 157.5 billion yuan in seven-day reverse repurchase operations and 300 billion yuan in overnight reverse repurchase operations on June 29, resulting in a net cash drain of 19 billion yuan. This marked the first actual implementation of overnight reverse repurchase operations. Notably, the PBOC disclosed only the volume of the overnight operation, not the interest rate. Market participants believe this reflects the PBOC's intention to manage market expectations and prevent overinterpretation of policy signals during the initial phase of the tool's deployment.

China Equities Markets

A-shares rose steadily, with the SSE Index closing up 1.16% at 4,073.90, the Shenzhen Component Index up 0.19%, the ChiNext Index up 0.54%, the Star 50 Index surging 4.61%, and the Wind All China Index up 0.71%. Market turnover reached 3.54 trillion yuan. AI hardware showed divergent trends: fiberglass, copper-clad laminate, and optical communications sectors adjusted, while silicon wafers and memory segments continued their strong rally. Innovative pharmaceuticals surged, with gains also seen in agriculture, food and beverage, non-bank financial services, and coal sectors.

Hong Kong equities closed sharply higher: the Hang Seng Index rose 1.57% to 23,026.68, the Hang Seng Tech Index jumped 3.23%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index gained 1.94%. Innovative pharmaceuticals led gains, followed by semiconductors and consumer sectors, while hardware equipment underperformed. Tech giants Tencent Holdings, Alibaba, and Meituan rebounded after prior underperformance. Net selling by Southbound funds exceeded 10.3 billion Hong Kong dollars, but Tencent was bought by over 1.5 billion Hong Kong dollars.

China Securities Index Company will launch the CSI Smart New Power System 100 Index on June 30, selecting 100 listed companies operating in six core sectors of the new power system: power generation, grid infrastructure, load management, energy storage, power system intelligence, carbon trading services, and equipment manufacturing.

The global top-performing memory ETF, Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM), recently rebalanced its portfolio, adding China's memory chip giant GigaDevice for the first time, with a weighting of 2.91%, making it the ETF's eighth-largest holding.

Sources say Baidu's AI chip subsidiary Kunlun Tech plans to list in Hong Kong with a target valuation of approximately 50 billion U.S. dollars. Sources indicate Tencent has become a customer of Kunlun Tech, and ByteDance is also considering its chips. However, individuals close to ByteDance said the company currently has no intention to collaborate with Kunlun.

China Industry News

Nearly 20 analog and power semiconductor companies globally are set to initiate a new round of price hikes on July 1, continuing a pattern of multi-stage, incremental adjustments this year. Many manufacturers report robust order backlogs and significantly improved capacity visibility.

Guangzhou issued its first fine for "hidden area" construction in real estate. The Liwan District Urban Management Bureau penalized a housing development project for illegally converting decorative columns into structural columns without planning approval, altering nearly 200 square meters.

China's National Healthcare Security Administration published a preliminary list of drugs that passed initial review for the 2026 drug catalog, with 557 and 54 drugs passing initial review for the basic medical insurance and commercial insurance innovation drug lists, respectively. The overall preliminary review pass rate was 92%.

Three national standards—"Safety Requirements for Electric Vehicles," "Safety Requirements for Power Batteries for Electric Vehicles," and "Occupant Protection in Side Collisions"—will take effect on July 1. Industry experts say the new standards will fundamentally shift automotive competition from price-based to value-based.

The National Supercomputing Internet Initiative launched the "10,000 GPU Collaborators Incentive Program," offering up to billion-token-scale computational resources and thousands of GPU-hours of domestic computing power. The program invites collaborative projects in AI and AI for Science, including best practices, paper readings, training livestreams, developer competitions, and AI application incubation.

Fintech

The Bank for International Settlements (BIS), in its latest annual report, warned that AI bubble bursting, inflation resurgence, and fiscal pressure are the three core threats to global prosperity. The report specifically highlighted risks in AI financing structures, particularly so-called "circular financing" arrangements.

Fintech unicorn Airwallex announced it raised $320 million in its H-round funding, valuing the company at $11 billion—a 38% increase from six months ago. The company is doubling down on AI-driven financial software.

Corporate News & Updates

The DeepSeek team announced that DeepSeek V4 will be officially launched in mid-July, introducing a peak-off-peak pricing strategy. During peak hours, API pricing will be double the standard rate, which remains unchanged from the current V4 pricing.

Ningde Times' Jiexiawo mine has received its safety production license. Industry insiders said all production-related permits have now been secured, with the critical safety license obtained on the 29th.

Elon Musk announced that xAI's latest large model, Grok 4.5, has entered beta testing internally at SpaceX and Tesla. Early evaluations suggest its performance is comparable to, or possibly exceeds, Anthropic's Claude Opus. xAI plans to release a new large model every month for the remainder of the year.

Australian AI infrastructure firm Firmus signed a strategic partnership with NVIDIA to build a dedicated 360-megawatt NVIDIA DSX AI factory campus on Batam Island, Indonesia. Firmus expects customer commitments to generate $25 billion to $30 billion in revenue.

Affected by disruptions in Middle Eastern operations and fierce competition in China, Toyota's global sales fell 7.2% year-over-year to 834,300 units, marking its fourth consecutive monthly decline. Sales in China dropped 31.7% to 102,300 units. Honda's global sales fell 4.9%, while Nissan's declined 10.3%.

Global media, entertainment, and telecommunications giant Comcast announced plans to spin off its media and telecommunications businesses into two independent, publicly traded companies via a tax-free separation, enabling each to pursue distinct strategic priorities.

U.S. commercial space company Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite communications operator Iridium Communications in a deal valued at approximately $8 billion. The acquisition will help the company bypass three major barriers to building a satellite communications business.

Global Macro & Policy

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to overturn a nearly 90-year-old administrative law precedent, significantly expanding the president's power to dismiss officers of independent federal agencies. The ruling permits Trump to remove FTC Democratic commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter, despite statutory restrictions requiring removal only for specific causes. Notably, the Court explicitly excluded the Federal Reserve from the ruling's scope, preserving its independence as previously established in May.

The U.S. Supreme Court denied President Trump's request to immediately remove Federal Reserve Governor Cook, but did not rule on whether Trump ultimately has the authority to dismiss Fed governors. Trump responded that he would ensure Cook does not make any significant monetary policy decisions.

Kevin Hassett, Director of the U.S. National Economic Council, said all available indicators suggest Thursday's nonfarm payrolls report will show "another strong number." He added that the surge in AI-driven productivity has a deflationary effect, reducing the justification for further rate hikes.

A joint working group from Oman and Iran held its first meeting on the Strait of Hormuz, exchanging views on its future management and related issues. Oman's Foreign Minister Badr stated Oman does not support charging transit fees for vessels but did not rule out exploring maritime service mechanisms. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Gharibbadi emphasized that all vessels must follow Iran's designated route through the Strait, warning that Iran will respond to any deviation.

Israeli Defense Minister Katz, in a closed-door meeting with military journalists, stated that the IDF is prepared to launch an independent military strike against Iran and reaffirmed Israel's intent to maintain a long-term military presence in southern Lebanon. He emphasized, however, that Israel will not interfere with President Trump's actions regarding Iran.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indexes closed higher: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.59% to 52,182.74, the S&P 500 gained 1.18% to 7,440.43, and the Nasdaq Composite surged 2.07% to 25,820.14. Caterpillar and Cisco led Dow gains, both up over 3%. Technology stocks drove the rally: the Wind US Magnificent Seven Index rose 2.05%, Tesla jumped over 8%, and Google rose nearly 5%. SpaceX gained over 7%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index rose 1.41%, Baidu surged over 7%, and Hesai Tech climbed over 6%.

European major indexes closed slightly lower: Germany's DAX fell 0.18% to 24,626.89, France's CAC 40 dropped 0.21% to 8,367.33, and the UK's FTSE 100 declined 0.23% to 10,484.22. Profit-taking due to elevated tech valuations and uncertainty around Fed policy weighed on market sentiment.

Asian markets showed mixed performance: Japan's Nikkei 225 closed up 0.15% at 69,468.11. South Korea's KOSPI fell 0.2% to 8,394.65, but the KOSDAQ surged 8.13%, briefly triggering a circuit breaker. Foreign investors sold a record 7.7 trillion won in Korean equities on Monday. India's S&P BSE Sensex declined 0.48% to 76,728.37.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market remained strong, with yields on major government bonds declining across the curve, with one-year yields falling about 3 basis points. Treasury futures rallied, with the 30-year contract up 0.12%. Market liquidity remained comfortably loose, with the PBOC net draining 19 billion yuan. The debut of overnight reverse repos sparked speculation that the rate may be set far below prior market expectations.

U.S. Treasury yields moved mixed: the two-year yield rose 2.26 basis points to 4.106%, the three-year yield rose 2.53 basis points to 4.107%, the five-year yield rose 1.92 basis points to 4.143%, the 10-year yield rose 1.17 basis points to 4.374%, and the 30-year yield dipped 0.10 basis point to 4.863%.

Commodities

International precious metals futures generally fell: COMEX gold futures dropped 1.61% to $4,030.50 per ounce, and COMEX silver futures fell 1.54% to $58.76 per ounce. The Supreme Court's ruling bolstering Fed independence, combined with hawkish Fed signals reinforcing tightening expectations, pressured precious metals prices, with only limited support from geopolitical uncertainty.

U.S. crude oil futures rose 1.72% to $70.42 per barrel; Brent crude rose 1.34% to $73.57 per barrel. Escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran, coupled with slowed energy transit through the Strait of Hormuz, led markets to reassess assumptions of rapid supply recovery. Actual supply was constrained by vessel congestion, and futures had been oversold, pushing prices higher.

Most base metals on the LME closed lower: LME zinc rose 0.43% to $3,487.00 per tonne; LME copper fell 0.10% to $13,344.50; LME tin fell 0.40% to $50,350.00; LME lead fell 0.58% to $1,892.50; LME nickel fell 1.79% to $16,400.00; LME aluminum fell 2.55% to $3,098.50.

Russian President Putin, during a meeting on domestic fuel market stability, said systemic measures are needed to increase fuel supply and maintain economically reasonable prices. He also indicated the government is considering a full ban on diesel exports.

Wood Mackenzie forecasts that EU storage facilities may reach only 76% capacity by the end of the key gas refill season (April to October), the lowest peak level since at least 2011, according to data from the European Gas Infrastructure Organization.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi closed at 6.7929 per U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 68 basis points from the previous close, and settled at 6.7960 in the night session. The PBOC's mid-rate was set at 6.8175, a 9-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York close, the U.S. dollar index fell 0.24% to 101.11. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro gained 0.34% to 1.1424, the pound rose 0.48% to 1.3260, the Australian dollar fell 0.12% to 0.6887, the dollar-yen rose 0.12% to 161.9360, the dollar-loonie rose 0.12% to 1.4211, the dollar-franc fell 0.29% to 0.8075, and offshore renminbi gained 40 basis points to 6.8009.

The yen fell to 161.97 per U.S. dollar, breaching the 161.95 level at which Japan intervened in July 2024, marking the lowest level since December 1986. Japan's record $780 billion intervention a month ago has now been fully unwound.

This Morning's Top Story

The macro landscape is locking down into a high-stakes waiting game today, June 29, 2026, as institutional desks brace for tomorrow's massive, dual-pronged USDA data drop. The entire financial complex is at a near-standstill ahead of the June 30 Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. 

This landmark release is expected to completely reset the baseline supply math for the remainder of the calendar year, forcing automated trend-following models and commodity trading advisors (CTAs) to run highly defensive, lightened books into the end of the quarter.

Exacerbating this end-of-month caution is a rapid cooling of consumer demand metrics across the physical livestock sector. Last week's post-peace-accord equity melt-up has officially met structural retail resistance. Friday afternoon's cold storage data revealed that domestic beef stocks failed to register their typical late-spring drawdown. 

This clear signal of slowing consumer clearance paired with a sharp breakdown in premium wholesale cutout values—has heavily dampened recent supply-side panic, sending meat futures into a corrective retreat as the market tests reality past the upcoming July 4th grilling peak.

Market Pulse & Strategy
The smart money is moving with absolute seasonal discipline this afternoon. Institutional managers are executing aggressive profit-taking sweeps across livestock length, recognizing that the combination of sticky cold storage inventories and collapsing choice boxes signals an immediate, near-term demand ceiling. In the agricultural room, a total structural gridlock has taken hold. Commercial end-users and international grain buying houses have pulled their bids completely, content to stand entirely aside until tomorrow's noon government report establishes the true physical balance sheet for the 2026/27 marketing year.

Pro Takeaway: The demand side of the ledger is reasserting its authority over supply metrics. While the cattle countryside is structurally thin, yesterday's carcass values prove that packers will aggressively throttle processing speeds the second wholesale margins compress. Keep your trailing defensive hedges locked down tightly, and do not make the mistake of front-running tomorrow's high-stakes acreage numbers.

The paper market is putting on a masterclass in risk management today. Seeing August Feeders get smacked back below $370 proves exactly what we suspected—the board simply cannot outrun a five-dollar collapse in boxed beef values. Packers are looking at a backup in cold storage and fading retail margins, and they are going to use that exact excuse to slow down cash cattle trade this week. Over in the grain room, July Corn is just staring at the wall at $4.13. It is a completely dead, un-tradeable tape until the USDA drops the hammer tomorrow at noon. Keep your powder dry.

Notes

The Cold Storage Reality Check: The quiet macro story of the weekend is the USDA’s latest cold storage data, which showed that total U.S. beef inventories for May failed to track their historical late-spring drawdown. This confirms that despite lower slaughter counts, high retail prices are finally forcing the domestic consumer to ration steak and premium cuts. For Western Corn Belt operations holding heavy, long-fed inventories, this demand drag means packer patience is going to intensify as we enter the mid-summer slowdown.

The Rest of the Week

Tuesday, June 30 (12:00 PM Eastern): The blockbuster USDA Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. This will trigger massive, automated volume spikes across all grain sessions.

Wednesday, July 1: Weekly EIA crude oil and ethanol inventory datasets, tracking domestic blending metrics against a sub-$71 crude baseline.

Trader Strategy: Maintain maximum defensive stop parameters on all remaining long grain layers, leaving your baseline accounts completely clear ahead of tomorrow's acreage volatility. In livestock, pull your trailing stop-losses up aggressively to insulate the core profits gained from last week's rally, keeping your expectations firmly aligned with a slower, packer-controlled country cash trade.

text:
11,000 Brazilian cattle just transited through Syrian territory to Iraq — via Tartus port and the al-Tanf crossing.

50,000 more are coming.

The al-Tanf crossing was closed for over a decade — held by various factions through ISIS control, US-backed forces, and finally the Syrian Desert Security Forces. It only reopened to Iraqi oil tanker trucks in March 2026. Now it's processing live cattle shipments too.

This is not oil, not gas, not construction machinery. This is livestock — a logistics category with far stricter requirements than almost any cargo: veterinary inspection, cold chain integrity, animal welfare standards, customs, laboratories, and quality control coordination, according to Syria's port authority.

A Syrian port official put it directly: "the arrival of this shipment clearly reflects renewed confidence in Syrian ports."

Brazilian cattle exporters don't choose a transit route casually. They chose Tartus-to-al-Tanf-to-Iraq because someone calculated it reliable enough for a 50,000-head contract.

Connect this to what's already running through the same crossing: Iraqi oil tanker trucks crossing al-Tanf overland en route to the Baniyas terminal — 299 tanker trucks crossed in a single week in April. Heavy machinery arriving at Latakia. Highway rehabilitation linking Nasib to Aleppo.

Syria's ports and crossings aren't just reopening for energy and construction. They're becoming trusted nodes in agricultural supply chains spanning from South America to Mesopotamia.

A crossing closed for over a decade is now moving cattle and oil trucks. The corridor works in both directions.
